summary: The Carrboro Town Council passed a resolution to support immigrant communities and 4th Amendment rights, committing to foster a welcoming environment, advocate for clear paths to citizenship and protection of rights, support access to municipal services regardless of immigration status, empower law enforcement to build trust, and encourage community dialogue and cultural exchange.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town Clerk's office is committed to providing high-quality customer service and encourages residents to contact or visit for questions or assistance with specific information or projects.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Carrboro has various active and inactive advisory boards and committees, including the Planning Board, Transportation Advisory Board, Environmental Advisory Board, and Greenways Commission, which are involved in the planning process for the town.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town Manager's office is responsible for the administration and execution of all town affairs, implementing policies directed by the Town Council, coordinating and supervising departments, advising the Council, and developing a leadership team to promote progressive and innovative local government and provide administrative support.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town Manager is authorized to execute various contracts and amendments, including a Stormwater and Utility Rate Study Contract, a contract with a Design-Build Firm for Town Hall renovation, and an amendment with the Town Engineer for engineering services.

This topic describes the history of Carrboro, from its settlement in 1882 as West End, its incorporation as Venable in 1911, and its renaming to Carrboro in 1913. It highlights the town's growth and its reputation as a progressive community, being the first in NC to elect an openly gay Mayor and grant domestic-partner benefits.

The Carrboro Town Council receives regular updates on major transportation infrastructure projects, which are managed by various entities and are in different stages of development, affecting Carrboro residents.

Carrboro is undertaking several public works projects including downtown sidewalk improvements to address trip hazards and enhance pedestrian safety, a 2025 Street Resurfacing Project to improve 27,574 square yards of roadways and install a new sidewalk on Roberson St, and the approved Westwood Cemetery Master Plan which includes expanded burial areas, new road access, walking paths, a rain garden, and other enhancements.

Carrboro is recognized as a Silver Level Bicycle Friendly Community and promotes biking through initiatives like Bike Month events, workshops, and support for bike-friendly infrastructure and policies, aiming to reduce car use and emissions and promote healthy living, as outlined in plans like the Carrboro Connects Comprehensive Plan and Safe Routes to School Strategic Action Plan.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Information Technology department aims to provide cost-effective technology-based solutions to enhance town services, improve access to vital government data, streamline business processes, and increase business continuity by partnering with departments and external organizations.

Carrboro is evaluating the permanent closure of East Weaver Street to vehicles to enhance place-making and the bike-ped network. A capacity study was conducted, and the Town Council paused the project for a year for further community engagement. Recommendations include lane reallocation on East Main and North Greensboro Streets and maintaining Maple Avenue as one-way.

Carrboro Earth Day is an event celebrating sustainability with eco-friendly activities, outdoor fun, and learning opportunities to protect the planet. It features car-free streets, cycling, walking, green initiatives, and various vendors and organizations. Moving forward, Carrboro Day and Earth Day will be combined into one event.

Carrboro Public Works is working to restore full solid waste collection services, especially in areas affected by flood damage. They are utilizing a fleet of vehicles, including borrowed side loaders and trucks from neighboring counties, to collect debris, dumpsters, and bulk waste, prioritizing hard-hit areas.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative concerns the management and accessibility of the Town Directory for various departments and boards (e.g., Board of Adjustment, Planning Board staff) on the government website, including features like 'Live Edit' functionality.

Hillsborough provides comprehensive waste collection services including Christmas trees, loose leaves (seasonal), monthly bulk items, and weekly household garbage. The town outlines specific rules for items, placement, and fees for special collections. It also promotes food waste composting and offers special garbage collection for residents with disabilities.

Hillsborough implements policies and regulations for backflow prevention devices to protect its public water supply from contamination. This includes requirements for installation, annual testing, winter protection, and strict protocols for reporting and penalizing tampering or theft of these devices, which are crucial for public safety.

The Hillsborough Police Department has established a policy for the use and management of body-worn and in-car camera systems. This policy covers training, deployment, activation and deactivation procedures, exceptions for privacy, and strict guidelines for record management, retention, and release, aiming to build public trust and document interactions.

Hillsborough's Stormwater and Environmental Services Division works to reduce stormwater runoff pollution through permit compliance, development regulation, and public education. Initiatives include enforcing riparian buffers, managing private stormwater control measures (like ponds with goose deterrence), addressing illegal dumping, and offering educational programs and partnerships to protect the environment.

Hillsborough levies a 1% prepared food and beverage sales tax and a 3% hotel/motel room occupancy tax. Proceeds fund the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority, which reinvest in the Visitors Center, grants, special projects, and partnerships to expand tourism and encourage lodging stays. The town also offers funding opportunities for nonprofit agencies.

Hillsborough manages various parks and greenways, including Gold Park and Riverwalk Greenway, with policies for flood resilience, severe weather closures, and providing shade (tree planting). The Public Space and Sustainability Division oversees these areas, and the Parks and Recreation Board advises on improvements and master plan implementation.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: The Town of Hillsborough is undertaking a rewrite of its Unified Development Ordinance (UDO) to align with the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. This update will set standards for quality development and land use, incorporating sustainability, economic resilience, and equity goals. Key priorities include keeping zoning consistent with plans, streamlining permitting, encouraging various housing types (including ADUs), reducing surface parking, adopting electric vehicle charging requirements, updating sidewalk and signage regulations, and increasing multimodal accessibility. The project involves public review and comment opportunities.

The Hillsborough Police Department offers and requires various training programs for its officers, including Basic Law Enforcement Training (BLET) for entry-level officers (which can be sponsored by the department), field training for new hires, annual mandatory in-service training (e.g., domestic violence, ethics, firearms, legal updates, proactive wellness), and elective training. The department also provides specialized training in Jiu Jitsu, virtual reality scenarios, crisis intervention, leadership, and professional certification, aiming to develop skills, reduce use of force, and improve community engagement.

The Hillsborough Police Department's Operations Division performs patrol duties and community policing, including responding to 911 calls, high-visibility patrol, proactive traffic enforcement, and working with a social worker to assist individuals and divert them from the criminal justice system, especially those with mental illness or who are homeless. The department also conducts alcohol law enforcement, educates drivers on traffic safety, and participates in state highway safety campaigns to reduce collisions (e.g., drinking and driving, seatbelts, speeding).

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
impacted_stakeholders: Planning and Economic Development Division staff
summary: The Hillsborough Historic District Commission oversees exterior changes within the Hillsborough Historic District, requiring a Certificate of Appropriateness or minor works approval for building, moving, demolishing, altering, or restoring structures. The commission's mission is to identify, protect, and preserve architectural resources, guided by design standards and matrices. The district, established in 1973, includes over 100 historic buildings and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town of Hillsborough defines its service provision and zoning jurisdiction, which extends to an extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) area beyond town limits. The town provides garbage collection, law enforcement, and water and sewer services within its limits, and may provide water and sewer services in the ETJ. For areas outside town limits, including the ETJ, Orange County and other agencies provide services like recycling, fire protection, and law enforcement. The town also has a courtesy review agreement with Orange County for development projects in the ETJ.

impacted_stakeholders: Orange County Department of Social Services
summary: Hillsborough is implementing several initiatives to alleviate the high cost of water and sewer services for customers. These include conducting a rates study (completed FY2024 for FY2025-2040), reducing the monthly minimum water usage charge from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons since 2013, operating a Water Assistance Program since 2006 for eligible customers, increasing system efficiency through innovation, and reducing the number of expensive-to-maintain pump stations. The town aims to balance operational costs, debt payments for infrastructure upgrades (e.g., wastewater plant, reservoir), and state regulations.

impacted_stakeholders: North Carolina Division of Water Resources
summary: The Hillsborough Utilities Department manages the town's water and wastewater treatment, preparing drinking water from the Eno River and cleaning used water before returning it to the environment. This includes maintaining pipes, water tanks, booster pump stations, and sewage lift stations. The department regularly tests for contaminants, publishes annual Water Quality and Wastewater Quality Reports, and offers tours of its treatment plants. It adheres to state and federal regulations, including those for Falls Lake, which require high treatment standards.

Hillsborough provides information and guidelines for residents and town operations to prepare for and respond to winter weather and cold temperatures. Town crews prepare equipment for snow and ice removal, and residents are advised on heater safety, carbon monoxide prevention, protecting vulnerable individuals and pets, creating warm rooms, and preventing frozen pipes. The town emphasizes staying informed, preparing emergency kits, and avoiding unnecessary travel during severe weather to ensure community safety and efficient emergency response.

The Hillsborough Police Department has a comprehensive "Use of Force Continuum" policy that guides officers in responding to resistance. The policy emphasizes using all reasonable options to gain voluntary compliance before resorting to force, using only necessary force, and de-escalating when possible. It defines various levels of force from verbal commands to deadly force, outlines legal authority, and specifies reporting, review, and investigation procedures for use-of-force incidents, including mandatory debriefings for officers involved in deadly force events. The policy also includes provisions for less-lethal weapons and specific prohibitions.

Hillsborough is actively implementing the revised EPA Lead and Copper Rule to protect public health from lead in drinking water. This includes developing a publicly available inventory of water service line materials by October 16, 2024, adding a corrosion inhibitor (orthophosphate) to the water, and conducting periodic water testing. The town also has a program to replace galvanized water mains. Residents are encouraged to help identify their private service line materials and are offered free internal water testing and tips to limit exposure.

Hillsborough manages special event permits and facility reservations for public events in parks, including requirements for permits, prohibited activities, application timelines, and zoning compliance for banners and signs. Events are limited to 75 people and parks close 30 minutes after sunset.

Hillsborough maintains drainage areas, rights of way, and utility easements, periodically mowing and clearing them to ensure water flow and utility access. Property owners are notified before work, are responsible for upkeep in easements and rights of way, and must maintain specific clearances around utility features. Easements give the town legal access for public uses and infrastructure work without removing property from owners.

Hillsborough enforces a 50-foot riparian buffer along streams and the Eno River to protect water quality, control erosion, reduce flooding, and provide habitat. The town removes large woody debris from streams and rivers on a case-by-case basis when it obstructs infrastructure, while recognizing its ecological benefits.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: Hillsborough regulates various work through permits, including driveway permits, utility cut permits, and stormwater management plans for new development. The town also enforces a 50-foot riparian buffer, requiring approval for any impact. Additionally, there's an alert about an email scam requesting permit fees.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Hillsborough offers multiple ways to pay utility bills, including online registration for viewing bills, setting up automatic payments via debit/credit cards or bank draft, and receiving text/email alerts. Payments can also be made through bank-originated bill pay, deposit box, in person, or mail. The town does not add fees for electronic payments.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative details the committee assignments and representative capacities of Hillsborough town council members, such as Mark Bell and Darab. These roles involve participation in various regional and local boards and groups, reflecting their engagement in different community focus areas.

Hillsborough, in partnership with Orange County, utilizes the OC Alerts mass notification system for emergency messages such as water/sewer emergencies, evacuations, and missing persons. Residents are encouraged to subscribe and keep their contact information updated to receive alerts based on their specified locations.

The Hillsborough Utilities Department is creating an inventory of water service line materials to identify lead service lines, as required by the EPA's Lead and Copper Rule. The town is seeking community help from property owners to identify unknown service line materials on the customer side, especially for homes built before 1987, using an online map and a material identification form.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Hillsborough conducts a community survey every two years to assess resident satisfaction with town services, compare performance, and guide decision-making and budgeting. The town has received the 'Leading the Way Award' for high resident satisfaction in 2021 and 2023, ranking in the top 10% of U.S. local governments.

Hillsborough is undertaking a sewer pipe relining project, which may cause temporary sewer service disruptions and an odor due to the curing process. Residents are advised on how to avoid odor, what to do if odor occurs, and how to prevent sewer backups during the project, which is expected to start in November 2025.

Hillsborough has a policy requiring the State Bureau of Investigation to conduct independent criminal investigations into incidents involving deadly force by department members resulting in death or serious injury. This ensures objectivity and integrity, with findings submitted to the district attorney. It also includes administrative investigations, vehicular-related investigations by NC State Highway Patrol, administrative leave for involved officers, and critical incident debriefing with a psychologist.

Hillsborough is undertaking two major infrastructure projects: adding a booster pump station to improve water supply interconnection and relocating the River Pumping Station from a floodplain to increase sewer capacity. Both projects sought Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (BRIC) grants from FEMA, totaling over $12.4 million in estimated costs, with FEMA grants potentially covering a significant portion. The BRIC program experienced temporary cancellation but has since been reestablished.

Hillsborough maintains water quality through a disinfection process using chlorine and ammonia to produce chloramines. The town conducts annual chlorine disinfection and periodic water system flushing to ensure high disinfection levels and remove sediment. The town also advises residents on issues related to whole-house filters, black residue from iron/manganese bacteria, and the importance of flushing water systems in underutilized buildings.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Hillsborough manages its finances through a three-year budget plan, with the first year being legally binding. The budget process involves staff proposals, retreats with elected officials, public hearings, and board votes, culminating in an approved budget effective July 1. The town operates with a General Fund, Stormwater Fund, and Water and Sewer Fund, and uses a strategic plan to align resources with priorities. Annual financial reports are also prepared and volunteer boards have their own budgets.

Hillsborough has a policy for supervisory and command review of all reportable use-of-force incidents. Supervisors conduct initial reviews, including scene observations, interviews, and documentation, forwarding findings to their commander. Division commanders then conduct a more comprehensive review, including past officer performance, and submit findings to the chief of police for final review. Potential policy violations or excessive force are immediately escalated to the commander or chief.

impacted_stakeholders: Hillsborough Public Space and Sustainability Division
summary: Hillsborough is a designated Bee City USA, committed to expanding pollinator-friendly practices and raising awareness about pollinators. Initiatives include maintaining pollinator gardens at various locations like Gold Park and downtown planters, with support from the Orange County Master Gardener Program. The town also provides resources for residents to create pollinator-friendly environments and coordinates with local beekeepers for honeybee swarms.

Hillsborough is working with state and railroad partners to reestablish passenger rail service and build a new train station on a 20-acre site. The project includes designing a 7,000 sq ft station building, passenger platform, parking, and connectivity improvements, with a conceptual plan already in place. The design and engineering phase has a budget of up to $800,000, funded through the State Transportation Improvement Program and agreements with N.C. Department of Transportation and GoTriangle.

The Hillsborough Public Works Division is responsible for the maintenance of town-owned streets, including pothole repairs and repaving. The town aims to repave about 5% of its roads annually, typically on a 20-year cycle, using town and state funds. An engineering firm is engaged every five years to assess road conditions and inform maintenance decisions.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
impacted_stakeholders: Orange County Affordable Housing Advisory Board
summary: Hillsborough residents can serve on several Orange County boards, including the Affordable Housing Advisory Board, Animal Services Advisory Board, Animal Services Hearing Panel Pool, and Human Relations Commission. Appointments to these boards are made by the Orange County Board of Commissioners, often with input from the Hillsborough Board of Commissioners, allowing Hillsborough residents to contribute to county-level policy and services.

Hillsborough has a tiered drought policy to restrict water use during periods of short supply from the Eno River, Lake Orange, or the town's reservoir. The policy outlines three stages: voluntary restrictions (10% reduction, <180 days supply), mandatory restrictions (10-20% reduction, no pressure washing/private car washing, limited outdoor watering, <135 days supply), and rationing (30% reduction, no outdoor water usage, irrigation meters turned off, <90 days supply). The town also takes measures like lowering water pressure and enhancing public awareness during these stages.
